title: Multilevel Understanding
description: Answer questions at three levels: literal, inferential and critical.

questions.question: [Podcast about climate change] "The speaker mentions that global temperatures have risen by 1.1°C." This is a ___ question.
questions.explanation: This is a literal question — it asks about a specific fact directly stated.

questions.question: [Podcast] "The speaker uses words like 'devastating' and 'urgent'. We can infer the speaker feels..."
questions.explanation: Inferential: word choice like "devastating" and "urgent" reveals strong concern.

questions.question: The first listening should focus on the general idea. This strategy is called listening for the ___.
questions.explanation: Listening for the gist = capturing the general topic and main idea on first listen.

questions.question: You hear: "To be honest, I think the plan won't work." The discourse marker "to be honest" signals...
options: A sincere personal opinion is coming
questions.explanation: "To be honest" signals the speaker is about to share their genuine, possibly unpopular opinion.

questions.question: You hear: "Many people think social media is harmless. However..." What comes next?
questions.explanation: "However" is a contrast marker — the speaker will present the opposite view.

questions.question: When you hear "actually" in a podcast, it often signals a ___ or emphasis. (correction/greeting)
questions.explanation: "Actually" often corrects a previous assumption or adds surprising information.

questions.question: Which listening strategy helps you prepare BEFORE listening?
questions.explanation: Predicting = anticipating content before listening by reading the title, looking at images, etc.

questions.question: You hear a word you don't know. The best strategy is to...
options: Stop and look it up immediately
Keep listening and guess from context
questions.explanation: Context clues: keep listening and use surrounding information to deduce meaning.

questions.question: "Overall, I believe that technology has improved our lives." The marker "overall" signals...
questions.explanation: "Overall" indicates the speaker is wrapping up with a final conclusion.

questions.question: English uses ___ markers like "firstly", "however", and "in fact" to organize spoken discourse.
questions.explanation: Discourse markers organize and signal the structure of spoken and written English.

title: Reconstruct the Summary
description: Build a summary by ordering the main ideas of the audio.

sentences.prompt: Summarize the podcast about climate change
sentences.correct: The podcast discussed solutions to climate change.
The speaker argued that individual action matters.
It concluded by calling for policy changes.
The experts agreed that technology enhances learning.

sentences.prompt: Build a summary about health benefits
sentences.correct: Regular exercise was described as essential for mental health.
The main recommendation was to reduce screen time.

title: Audio Vocabulary
description: Match words heard with their contextual meanings.

title: Discourse Markers
description: Learn speech markers that help you follow what you hear.

cards.back: Actually / In fact — Indicates correctness or surprising information. NO means "currently" (= currently).
cards.example: I thought it was expensive, but actually it was quite affordable.

cards.back: In fact — Reinforces or intensifies what was said before. Add weight.
cards.example: She's not just good at English. In fact, she's fluent in five languages.

cards.back: To be honest — Enter an honest, possibly controversial, personal opinion.
cards.example: To be honest, I don't think the project will succeed.

cards.back: Basically — Simplifies or summarizes something complex.
cards.example: Basically, we need more time and money to finish this.

cards.back: That is to say / I mean — Clarifies or rephrases what was said.
cards.example: It was okay, I mean, it wasn't terrible, but it wasn't great either.

cards.back: Apparently — Information that the speaker received but has not confirmed.
cards.example: Apparently, they are going to close the office next month.

cards.back: On the other hand — Introduces the opposite side of an argument.
cards.example: The job pays well. On the other hand, the hours are terrible.

cards.back: So basically — Enter an informal summary of what was discussed.
cards.example: So basically, we need to start from scratch.

cards.back: The thing is... — Introduce the most important point or obstacle.
cards.example: The thing is, we don't have enough budget for that.

cards.back: As I said... — Take up an interrupted point.
cards.example: As I was saying, the results were quite surprising.

title: The Podcast Studio
description: Explore the podcast studio and practice active listening stations.
